raw.fact: Shakuntala (Shaku) Atre is an Indian data scientist and American businesswoman.
She worked for IBM for 14 years before starting her own firm.
Atre published the book "Database: Structured Techniques for Design, Performance, and Management: With Case Studies" in 1980, which was one of the first books on managing databases.
She also co-authored the book "Business Intelligence Roadmap: The Complete Project Lifecycle for Decision-Support Applications" with Larissa Moss in 2003.
Atre has served as an adjunct professor of data science at the University of Pune and several institutions in the United States.
Her works, including her books, have been used as university textbooks.
Atre was born in 1940 in India and studied mathematics, statistics, and computer programming.
She moved to the United States in 1971 with her husband and began working for IBM.
Atre left IBM in 1981 to start her own consulting firm and later sold it to Computer Assistance, Inc. She then negotiated a joint-venture with Intec, Inc.
Atre has published several other books and articles and has been a contributing editor to the journal DBMS.

Butler W. Lampson is an American computer scientist known for his contributions to distributed personal computing.
His early education includes an A.B. in physics from Harvard University and a PhD in electrical engineering and computer science from the University of California, Berkeley.

Lampson was one of the founding members of Xerox PARC, where he worked as a principal scientist and senior research fellow, and helped design the Xerox Alto, the first personal computer with a graphical user interface (GUI).
Lampson also worked on many other influential technologies at PARC, such as laser printer design, Ethernet, and the Bravo program.
He later worked for Digital Equipment Corporation and Microsoft Research.
Lampson has received numerous honors and awards throughout his career, including the ACM Turing Award, the IEEE John von Neumann Medal, and the Charles Stark Draper Prize.
He is often quoted as saying, "Any problem in computer science can be solved with another level of indirection".
Lampson's website and an oral history interview with him are available online.

Luca Andrea Cardelli is an Italian computer scientist and research professor at the University of Oxford.
He is known for his research in type theory and operational semantics.
Cardelli helped design the language Modula-3 and implemented the first compiler for the functional language ML.
He defined the concept of typeful programming and helped develop the experimental language Polyphonic C#.
Cardelli received his PhD from the University of Edinburgh in 1982.
He previously worked for Bell Labs and Digital Equipment Corporation on Unix software including vismon.
Cardelli was inducted as a Fellow of the Association for Computing Machinery in 2004 and was elected a Fellow of the Royal Society in 2005.
In 2007, he was awarded the Senior AITO Dahl-Nygaard Prize named for Ole-Johan Dahl and Kristen Nygaard.

Don Estridge was an American computer engineer
He led the development of the original IBM Personal Computer (PC)
He is known as the "father of the IBM PC"
His decisions dramatically changed the computer industry, resulting in a vast increase in sales of personal computers, thus creating an entire industry of hardware manufacturers of IBM PCs.

Estridge completed a bachelor's degree in electrical engineering at the University of Florida and worked at the Army, designing a radar system using computers, IBM and finally NASA's Goddard Space Flight Center until he moved to Boca Raton, Florida, in 1969.
Before being the leader of the team to develop the IBM PC he had been the lead manager for the development of the IBM Series/1 mini-computer.
In mid-1980, he was given the opportunity to lead IBM's efforts in the emerging personal computer business.
The specifications of the IBM PC were published, allowing third-party hardware and software to be used.
He and his wife were killed in the crash of Delta Air Lines Flight 191 on August 2, 1985.
Estridge has been honored many times, and the Don Estridge High-Tech Middle School in Boca Raton is named after him.

Gillian Lovegrove is a retired computer scientist and academic.
Lovegrove was Dean of the School of Informatics at Northumbria University.
She was president of the Conference of Professors and Heads of Computing and a Higher Education consultant to the British Computer Society.
Lovegrove is known for her interest in gender imbalance in computer education and employment and for discussing solutions to a shortage of information technology graduates in the UK.
Lovegrove studied at Newnham College, Cambridge and started her career as a lecturer in mathematics at Portsmouth Polytechnic.
Lovegrove conducted research on object-oriented computing and co-wrote two papers about girls and computer education.
Lovegrove organised "Women into Computing" conferences in the late 1980s.
Lovegrove was associate dean and head of information systems at the University of Staffordshire's School of Computing.
Lovegrove gave evidence on the shortage of IT graduates to the Parliamentary Information Technology Committee in 2001 and proposed joint teaching schemes between employers and universities.
Lovegrove was head-hunted by Northumbria University in 1999 to be Head of the School of Informatics.

William Nelson Joy is an American computer engineer and venture capitalist
He co-founded Sun Microsystems in 1982 and served as Chief Scientist and CTO at the company until 2003
He played a key role in the development of BSD UNIX and was the original author of the vi text editor
Joy was elected a member of the National Academy of Engineering in 1999
He co-founded venture capital firm HighBAR Ventures in 1999, and joined Kleiner Perkins in 2005
In 2000, he wrote an essay titled "Why The Future Doesn't Need Us," expressing concerns over the risks of technological developments in genetics, nanotechnology, and robotics
Despite his concerns, Joy is a venture capitalist investing in GNR technology
Joy is credited with establishing "Joy's law," stating that most of the smartest people work for someone else, and that companies should hire the best employees
Joy also devised a formula in 1983 called Joy's law, stating that peak computer speed doubles each year, expressed in MIPS

Anders Peter Ravn was a Danish computer scientist born in Venezuela in 1947.

He received a Master of Science in Computer Science and Mathematics from the University of Copenhagen in 1973 and a Doctor of Technology in Computer Science from the Technical University of Denmark in 1995.

He was a teaching assistant at the University of Copenhagen, worked as a systems programmer at A/S Regnecentralen and held various academic positions at different institutions, including guest researcher at Oxford University and visiting professor at the University of Kiel in Germany.

Ravn specialized in research into formal methods, especially for embedded and hybrid systems. He worked on the development of duration calculus for real-time systems with Zhou Chaochen and Tony Hoare.
He was a member of the Dansk Selskab for Datalogi, the Association for Computing Machinery and the IEEE Computer Society.
Ravn received Ulrik and Marie Brinch's honorary award in 1996 and was honoured as a Knight of the Order of the Dannebrog in 2015.
Ravn produced many research papers and edited several books.
He died in Copenhagen in 2019.

Peter James Denning is an American computer scientist and writer.
He is best known for his pioneering work in virtual memory, especially for inventing the working-set model for program behavior.
Denning attended Manhattan College for a Bachelor in Electrical Engineering and then MIT for a PhD.
Denning has written several books and has published 340 technical papers.
He is also known for his works on principles of operating systems, computational models, and design and implementation of CSNET and ACM digital library.
Denning created a course on innovation as a set of learnable practices called Sense 21.
He has been a major influence in computer education and has contributed to the development of computing curriculum.
Denning is married to computer security expert Dorothy E. Denning.

James Gosling is a Canadian computer scientist and the founder and lead designer of the Java programming language.
He received a Bachelor of Science from the University of Calgary and his M.A. and Ph.D. from Carnegie Mellon University, all in computer science.
Gosling was with Sun Microsystems for 26 years and invented the NeWS windowing system and the Java programming language.

He left Sun Microsystems in 2010 after it was acquired by the Oracle Corporation and joined Google and later Liquid Robotics before transitioning to Amazon Web Services in 2017 as Distinguished Engineer.
Gosling has received numerous awards and is an advisor at the Scala company Lightbend, Independent Director at Jelastic, and Strategic Advisor for Eucalyptus.
He is also a board member of DIRTT Environmental Solutions and a fellow of the Association for Computing Machinery.
Gosling has authored several books, including "The Java Programming Language" and "The Java Language Specification."
His personal weblog and presentations are available online, and he has been interviewed on various topics related to computer science and innovation.

Bonnie Nardi is an emeritus professor at the University of California, Irvine and led the TechDec research lab in Human-Computer Interaction and computer-supported cooperative work.
Her work includes research on activity theory, interaction design, games, social media, and society and technology.
Nardi has worked for high-tech companies such as AT&T Labs, Agilent, Hewlett-Packard and Apple labs to examine consumers' behavior.
She has published several books on activity theory and interaction design, including "Acting with Technology" and "Activity Theory in HCI".
Nardi's research interests focus on human-computer interaction, computer-supported cooperative work, activity theory, computer-mediated communication, and interaction design.
She is widely known for her work on information ecologies, especially Chapter 7 of her book "Information Ecologies", which focused on librarians as keystone species.
Nardi holds a PhD from the School of Social Sciences at University of California, Irvine, and her self-described theoretical orientation is "activity theory".
She is currently conducting an ethnographic study of World of Warcraft.
Nardi has authored several papers published in academic journals, such as "NetWORKers and their activity in intensional networks" and "Why We Blog".
Her work has influenced digital anthropology, digital libraries, and other fields in computer science.
She was elected to the ACM CHI academy in 2013 and retired in 2018.

Andrew Vladislav Goldberg is an American computer scientist.
He primarily works on design, analysis, and experimental evaluation of algorithms.
He also worked on mechanism design, computer systems, and complexity theory.
Goldberg did his undergraduate studies at the Massachusetts Institute of Technology.
He earned a master's degree at the University of California, Berkeley, and finished his doctorate at MIT in 1987.
He was a faculty member at Stanford University and has worked for various organizations, including NEC Research Institute, Intertrust STAR Laboratories, Microsoft, and Amazon.com.
Goldberg is best known for his research on graph and network algorithms, including the maximum flow and shortest path problems.
He also worked on algorithmic game theory and was one of the first scientists to study worst-case mechanism design.
Goldberg has received numerous awards, including a Hertz Fellowship, A.W. Tucker Prize, NSF Presidential Young Investigator Award, and INFORMS Optimization Society Farkas Prize.
He is a fellow of the ACM and the Society for Industrial and Applied Mathematics.

Edward D. "Ed" Lazowska is an American computer scientist and Professor at the University of Washington.
His research focuses on high-performance computing, communication systems, and data-intensive science.
He has contributed to the development of approaches to kernel and system design in various areas, including thread management, load sharing, and cluster computing.
From 2008 to 2017, he was the Founding Director of the University of Washington eScience Institute.

Lazowska has held various leadership positions, including Chair of the Computing Research Association, the President’s Information Technology Advisory Committee, and the Computing Community Consortium.
He also serves as a board member or technical advisor for several technology-oriented organizations.
Lazowska has mentored many graduate and undergraduate students, including well-known figures in the field of computer science.
He has received numerous awards and recognition for his contributions to the field, including the Association for Computing Machinery Presidential Award and the ACM Distinguished Service Award.
Lazowska was born in Washington, D.C. in 1950 and earned his degrees from Brown University and the University of Toronto.
To celebrate Lazowska's 70th birthday, funds were raised to endow a set of Lazowska Professorships at the University of Washington.

Charles William Bachman III was an American computer scientist
He spent his entire career as an industrial researcher, developer, and manager rather than in academia
He was particularly known for his work in the early development of database management systems
His techniques of layered architecture include his namesake Bachman diagrams
Bachman was born in Manhattan, Kansas in 1924 and attended Michigan State College, where he graduated in 1948 with a bachelor's degree in mechanical engineering
He joined Dow Chemical in 1950 and became Dow's first data processing manager in 1957
In 1960, he joined General Electric and developed the Integrated Data Store (IDS), one of the first database management systems using the navigational database model
In 1983, Bachman founded Bachman Information Systems, which developed a line of computer-aided software engineering products, including the BACHMAN/Data Analyst
Bachman received the Turing Award from the Association for Computing Machinery (ACM) in 1973 and was elected as a Distinguished Fellow of the British Computer Society in 1977
He was awarded a National Medal of Technology and Innovation in 2012
Bachman papers from 1951 to 2007 are available from the Charles Babbage Institute at the University of Minnesota

Peter A. Wegner was a computer scientist who contributed to the theory of object-oriented programming and the Church-Turing thesis.
He received his education from the University of Cambridge and the University of London.
His seminal work in object-oriented programming is "On Understanding Types," co-authored with Luca Cardelli.
He co-authored papers and edited a book on the relevance of the Church-Turing thesis for empirical aspects of computer science.
Wegner was inducted as a Fellow of the ACM in 1995, received the ACM Distinguished Service Award in 2000, and the Austrian Cross of Honor for Science and Art, 1st class in 1999.
He suffered serious brain injuries when hit by a bus on his trip to London to receive his award but recovered after a lengthy coma.
He was the editor-in-chief of ACM Computing Surveys and The Brown Faculty Bulletin.
Wegner was a professor at Brown University.

Latanya Arvette Sweeney is an American computer scientist and a professor at Harvard University.
She is the founder and director of the Public Interest Tech Lab and the Data Privacy Lab.
Sweeney is credited with the observation that "87% of the U.S. population is uniquely identified by date of birth, gender, postal code".
She completed her undergraduate degree in computer science at the Harvard University Extension School and received her Ph.D. from the Massachusetts Institute of Technology.
Sweeney founded the Data Privacy Lab at Carnegie Mellon University in 2001.
Her research has focused on data privacy and re-identification, which includes her successful identification of then Massachusetts governor, William Weld, using publicly accessible records.
Her work has had a significant impact on privacy-centered policymaking, including the health privacy legislation HIPAA.
Sweeney's research aims to provide a cross-disciplinary perspective about privacy in the process of disseminating data.
In 2021, Sweeney launched the Public Interest Technology Lab at Harvard's John F. Kennedy School of Government.

Hans Zantema is a Dutch mathematician and computer scientist.
He is a professor at Radboud University in Nijmegen.
Zantema received his PhD in algebraic number theory at the University of Amsterdam in 1983.
He worked in the computer science industry from 1987 to 2000 at Utrecht University and since 2000 at Eindhoven University of Technology.
Zantema's main achievements are in term rewriting systems, specifically in proving termination.
He is known for Zantema's problem, which asks if the string rewrite system 0011 -> 111000 terminates.
Selected publications include his PhD thesis and articles on termination of term rewriting.
Zantema's homepage can be found on the website of his university.

Edward William Felten is a computer science and public affairs professor at Princeton University.
He was the Director of the Center for Information Technology Policy from 2007 to 2015 and from 2017 to 2019.
Felten was named Chief Technologist for the Federal Trade Commission in 2010 and Deputy U.S. Chief Technology Officer in 2015.
His research involves computer security and technology policy.
Felten was a witness in the United States v. Microsoft case where he demonstrated a tool to remove Internet Explorer without causing any problems with the operating system.
He is perhaps best known for his work on the Secure Digital Music Initiative (SDMI) challenge to break digital audio watermark technologies.

Felten, with help from the Electronic Frontier Foundation, sued SDMI and other copyright holders, requesting a declaratory judgment ruling that the publication of their scientific paper would be legal.
He discovered security flaws in a Diebold Election Systems voting machine and Sequoia Advantage voting machines.
In February 2008, Felten was part of the team that discovered the cold boot attack, which allows someone with physical access to a computer to bypass operating system protections.
Felten was named the first Chief Technologist of the Federal Trade Commission in 2010.

Hussein S. M. Zedan was a computer scientist of Egyptian descent, based mainly in the United Kingdom.
He received his PhD degree in 1981 at the University of Bristol, studying under John Derwent Pryce and Hubert Schwetlick.

Zedan was an academic in the Department of Computer Science at the University of York and later headed the Software Technology Research Laboratory (STRL) as Technical Director at De Montfort University.
He was also Head of Computing Research and subsequently appointed Assistant Vice-President of Academic Affairs and Development at the Applied Science University in Manama, Bahrain, until 2017.
Hussein Zedan died on 23 February 2019. He was married with two daughters.
Zedan published several papers, focusing on topics such as distributed computer systems, real-time systems, and formal specifications and testing.

Zedan was an academic in the Department of Computer Science at the University of York and later headed the Software Technology Research Laboratory (STRL) as Technical Director at De Montfort University.
He was also Head of Computing Research and subsequently appointed Assistant Vice-President of Academic Affairs and Development at the Applied Science University in Manama, Bahrain, until 2017.
Hussein Zedan died on 23 February 2019. He was married with two daughters.
Zedan published several papers, focusing on topics such as distributed computer systems, real-time systems, and formal specifications and testing.
James William Cooley was an American mathematician.
He received his B.A. degree from Manhattan College and Ph.D. degree in applied mathematics from Columbia University.
He worked on John von Neumann's computer, where he programmed the Blackman–Tukey transformation.
He co-developed the fast Fourier transform with John Tukey while working for the research division of IBM in 1965.
Dr. Richard L. Garwin at IBM Watson Research provided the motivation for this development to verify a nuclear arms treaty with the Soviet Union.
Cooley was a member of the Digital Signal Processing Committee of the IEEE, received the IEEE Centennial Medal, and in 2002, he received the IEEE Jack S. Kilby Signal Processing Medal.
He contributed to establishing the terminology in digital signal processing.
Cooley published various papers, including the Numerov-Cooley method for numerically solving one-dimensional Schrödinger equations.
He is listed as a pioneer in computer science.
His profile is available on the Mathematics Genealogy Project.

Neil Gunther is a computer information systems researcher known for developing the open-source performance modeling software and the Guerrilla approach to computer capacity planning and performance analysis.
He has made contributions to the field of large transients in computer systems and packet networks, and his universal law of computational scalability.
Gunther is a Senior Member of various computing organizations, including ACM, IEEE, AMS, APS, and CMG.
He was born in Melbourne in 1950 and has a background in physics and quantum mechanics.
Gunther has worked for companies such as Xerox PARC and Pyramid Technology, where he developed performance analysis software and attained high TPC benchmarks on Unix multiprocessors.

He founded Performance Dynamics Company in 1994 to provide consulting and educational services for high-performance computer systems and developed his own open-source performance modeling software called "PDQ."
Gunther is currently focused on developing quantum information system technologies and has developed a theory of photon bifurcation.
He has also worked on performance visualization tools and has co-founded the PerfViz google group.
Gunther has written several books on performance analysis and has received various awards and recognitions for his work.

Marvin Lee Minsky was an American cognitive and computer scientist, co-founder of the Massachusetts Institute of Technology's AI laboratory, and author of several texts concerning AI and philosophy.

Minsky received many accolades and honors, including the 1969 Turing Award, Golden Plate Award of the American Academy of Achievement, Japan Prize, IJCAI Award, Benjamin Franklin Medal, and Dan David Prize for "Artificial Intelligence, the Digital Mind".

Minsky's contributions to computer science include inventions such as the first head-mounted graphical display and the confocal microscope, the first Logo "turtle", the first randomly wired neural network learning machine, and several AI models.
His book Perceptrons attacked the work of Frank Rosenblatt and became the center of a controversy in the history of AI, as some claim it discouraged research into neural networks in the 1970s.
Minsky also developed the Society of Mind theory, which attempts to explain how intelligence could be a product of the interaction of non-intelligent parts.
Minsky was an adviser on Stanley Kubrick's movie 2001: A Space Odyssey and is mentioned in Arthur C. Clarke's derivative novel of the same name.
Minsky married pediatrician Gloria Rudischin in 1952, and together they had three children. He was an atheist and a signatory to the Scientists' Open Letter on Cryonics.

Minsky received a $100,000 research grant from Jeffrey Epstein in 2002, and organized two academic symposia on Epstein's private island Little Saint James. Minsky's widow denies allegations of any inappropriate behavior.
Minsky died of a cerebral hemorrhage in January 2016, and may or may not have been cryonically preserved by the Alcor Life Extension Foundation.

Minsky was affiliated with several organizations, including the United States National Academy of Engineering, National Academy of Sciences, and Alcor Life Extension Foundation's Scientific Advisory Board.

Philip Mayne Woodward was a British mathematician, radar engineer, and horologist
Woodward had notable success in mathematics, radar engineering, and horology
Woodward's achievements in radar include developing a mathematical beam-shaping technique and evaluating the ambiguities inherent in all radar signals
Woodward's achievements in horology include building W5, a clock acclaimed for its accuracy and design, and contributing to scientific horology with major analyses of balance springs and pendulums
Woodward was also an Honorary Professor in Electrical Engineering at the University of Birmingham and a Deputy Chief Scientific Officer at the Royal Signals and Radar Establishment
Woodward was born on September 6, 1919, and died on January 30, 2018, at the age of 98.

Clifford "Cliff" B. Jones is a British computer scientist.
He specializes in research into formal methods.
He proposed an extension to Hoare logic for handling concurrent programs, rely/guarantee, in his DPhil thesis at Oxford University Computing Laboratory.
He worked for IBM, where he contributed to the development of the Vienna Development Method (VDM).
He was a professor at the Victoria University of Manchester.
He also worked in the industry at Harlequin for a period and is now a Professor of Computing Science at Newcastle University.
He has been the editor-in-chief of the Formal Aspects of Computing journal.
He has authored and edited many books, including Understanding Programming Languages, VDM: Une methode rigoureuse pour le development du logiciel, and Systematic Software Development using VDM.
Jones is also interested in interdisciplinary aspects of computer science and the history of computer science.

Edwin Ellis (Ed) Tozer is a retired British management and IT consultant and SF author.
He started his career in the manufacturing and mining industry before becoming a junior systems analyst at Scicon in London in 1969.
In 1984, he founded Edwin E. Tozer Limited, which specialized in data management study and dissolved after his retirement in 2008.
Tozer is known for his early work on business information systems in the 1970s and 1980s.
He wrote several books, including "Application Development Tools" (1984), "Planning for Effective Business Information Systems" (1988), and "Strategic Is/It Planning" (1996).
Tozer was one of the first to propose the concept of business architecture in the field of business information systems planning.

His model for developing information systems consisted of four phases: determining business information needs, developing conceptual architecture for systems and data, determining key priorities and system groupings, and migration planning.
Tozer also wrote science fiction, including "Morphosis" (2010), where he speculated on the evolution of mankind as a species.

Robert Lamb Sproull was an American educator, physicist, and US Department of Defense official.
He was born in Lacon, Illinois, and graduated from Deep Springs College.
Sproull studied English literature at Cornell University before obtaining a Ph.D. in Physics from the same university.
He headed the Laboratory of Atomic and Solid State Physics (LASSP) and the Materials Science Center at Cornell University.
Sproull left Cornell to become the director of the Advanced Research Projects Agency (ARPA).
He served as provost and vice president of the University of Rochester, becoming its president in 1970.
He retired as president and became a professor of physics at the university in 1985.
Sproull was known by the nickname "Particle Bob" during his presidency at the University of Rochester.
He was a member of the Roundtable Council of the National Academy of Sciences and the chairman of the Defense Science Board.
In 2005, the University of Rochester named its Center for High Intensity Laser Research in his honor.
Sproull, with his wife, donated $1 million to create the Robert L. and Mary L. Sproull Fund in 2006 to support the work of the Cornell Center for Materials Research (CCMR).
He was awarded the Telluride Tech Festival Award of Technology in Telluride, Colorado, in 2006.
Sproull authored a book titled "Modern Physics."

Ken Kennedy was an American computer scientist and professor at Rice University
He was the founding chairman of Rice's Computer Science Department
Kennedy directed the construction of several substantial software systems for programming parallel computers
He wrote over 200 articles and book chapters, plus numerous conference addresses
Kennedy was elected to the National Academy of Engineering in 1990 and named a Fellow of the AAAS in 1994 and of the ACM and IEEE in 1995
In 1999, he was named recipient of the ACM SIGPLAN Programming Languages Achievement Award
Kennedy died of pancreatic cancer in Houston at the age of 61
On November 18, 2009, the ACM and IEEE awarded the first Ken Kennedy CS Award to Francine Berman of Rensselaer Polytechnic Institute
His last publication was about the failure of the High Performance Fortran language which he had championed
Kennedy directed the PhD dissertations of 38 graduate students and masters theses for 8 students.

Manindra Agrawal is a professor at the Indian Institute of Technology, Kanpur.
He received the first Infosys Prize for Mathematics, the Godel Prize in 2006, and the Shanti Swarup Bhatnagar Award in Mathematical Sciences in 2003.
Agrawal and his co-authors created the AKS primality test, winning the 2006 Fulkerson Prize and the 2006 Gödel Prize.
He was also awarded the 2002 Clay Research Award for this work.
The AKS primality test is the first unconditional deterministic algorithm to test an n-digit number for primality in a time that has been proven to be polynomial in n.
Agrawal was chosen for the first Infosys Mathematics Prize in 2008.
He served on the Mathematical Sciences jury for the Infosys Prize in 2014 and 2015.
Agrawal was a visiting scholar at the Institute for Advanced Study in 2003-04.
He was honored with the Padma Shri in 2013 and the TWAS Prize in 2010.

Martin Richards is a British computer scientist known for his development of the BCPL programming language.
BCPL was part of early research into portable software and is the ancestor of the B programming language invented by Ken Thompson in early versions of Unix.
Dennis Ritchie used BCPL as the basis of his widely used C programming language.
Richards studied mathematics and earned a PhD in programming language design and implementation from the University of Cambridge.
He was a senior lecturer at the University of Cambridge Computer Laboratory until his retirement in 2007.
Richards also developed the TRIPOS portable operating system.
He was awarded the IEEE Computer Society's Computer Pioneer Award in 2003.
Richards is a fellow of St John's College at the University of Cambridge.

Nils John Nilsson was an American computer scientist and one of the founding researchers in the discipline of artificial intelligence.
He was the first Kumagai Professor of Engineering in computer science at Stanford University from 1991 until his retirement.
Nilsson's contributions include search, planning, knowledge representation, and robotics.
He co-led a research team in the construction of Shakey, a robot that constructed a model of its environment from sensor data and reasoned about that environment to arrive at a plan of action.
Nilsson was the fourth President of the AAAI and a Founding Fellow of that organization.
He authored several books on AI, including Principles of Artificial Intelligence and Logical Foundations of Artificial Intelligence.
In 2011, he was inducted into IEEE Intelligent Systems' AI's Hall of Fame.
Nilsson died on April 23, 2019, at the age of 86.

Jonathan Andrew Crowcroft is the Marconi Professor of Communications Systems at the University of Cambridge.
He received a bachelor's degree in physics from the University of Cambridge and a master's degree in computing and a PhD from University College London.
Crowcroft has contributed to successful start-up projects and has served on the advisory board of Max Planck Institute for Software Systems.
His research has focused on theoretical network science, including the idea of using specially-built hardware for packet switching and optical technology.
Crowcroft was elected a Fellow of the Royal Society in 2013 and has received numerous other awards and honours throughout his career.
He served as general chair for the ACM SIGCOMM conference between 1995 and 1999 and received the SIGCOMM Award in 2009.

James Nicholas Gray was an American computer scientist who received the Turing Award in 1998 for his contributions to database and transaction processing research and technical leadership in system implementation.
Gray was born in San Francisco and spent most of the first three years of his life in Rome, Italy, where he learned to speak Italian before English.
Gray pursued his career by working as a researcher and software designer at a number of industrial companies, including IBM, Tandem Computers, and DEC. He joined Microsoft in 1995.
Gray is known for his contributions to major database and transaction processing systems, including SQL relational databases that have become a standard throughout the world.

Gray disappeared on January 28, 2007, during a short solo trip to scatter his mother's ashes at the Farallon Islands near San Francisco. His boat was not found, and he was declared legally dead on January 28, 2012.
Microsoft Research presents the Jim Gray eScience Award each year to a researcher who has made an outstanding contribution to the field of data-intensive computing.

Olaf O. Storaasli is a researcher and VP at Synective Labs.
He was previously a researcher at Oak Ridge National Laboratory and USEC, and also had a career at NASA.
He led the development of one of NASA's first parallel computers, the Finite element machine, and developed rapid matrix equation algorithms for high-performance computers.
He has mentored 25 NHGS students, and is recognized by American Men and Women of Science, Marquis Who's Who, and NASA, Cray, Intel and Concordia College awards.
Storaasli has a B.A. in Physics, Mathematics & French, an M.A. in Mathematics, and a Ph.D. in Engineering Mechanics.
His research involves developing parallel analysis software for matrix equation solutions to simulate physical and biological behavior on advanced-computer architectures.
Storaasli has published various books and papers on large-scale analysis, design, and parallel computational methods.
References to his work can be found in the Mathematics Genealogy Project, Intel Supercomputing Pioneers, and NASA Lessons Learned.

John Clifford Shaw was a systems programmer at the RAND Corporation.
He coauthored the first artificial intelligence program, the Logic Theorist.
He helped develop General Problem Solver and Information Processing Language.
Shaw is considered the true "father" of the JOSS language.
He worked with Allen Newell and Herbert A. Simon on the concept of list processing.
Shaw invented the linked list, which is fundamental in modern computing technology.

Gábor Tardos is a Hungarian mathematician.
He is currently a professor at Central European University and was previously a Canada Research Chair at Simon Fraser University.
Tardos works mainly in combinatorics and computer science.

He received his PhD in Mathematics from Eötvös University, Budapest in 1988 and held postdoctoral posts at the University of Chicago, Rutgers University, University of Toronto, and the Princeton Institute for Advanced Study.

Tardos received several awards, including the European Mathematical Society prize for young researchers, the Prize of the Hungarian Academy of Sciences for Young Researchers, the Erdős Prize from the Hungarian Academy of Sciences, and the Alfréd Rényi Prize.

He received a Lendület Grant from the Hungarian Academy of Sciences in 2009, and in 2020, he received the Gödel Prize for the algorithmic version of the Lovász local lemma that he developed with Robin Moser.
Tardos is the younger brother of Éva Tardos, who is also a renowned mathematician.

Tardos worked on several mathematical results, including exhibiting a maximal clone of order-preserving operations that is not finitely generated, obtaining partial results concerning the Hanna Neumann conjecture, and proving a combinatorial conjecture of Zoltán Füredi and Péter Hajnal that was known to imply the Stanley–Wilf conjecture.
He also developed a method for optimal probabilistic fingerprint codes and worked out an algorithm that is easy to implement.
Tardos was an invited speaker at the International Congress of Mathematicians in Rio de Janeiro in 2018.

His selected publications include papers on optimal probabilistic fingerprint codes, transversals of 2-intervals, on the power of randomization in on-line algorithms, and a maximal clone of monotone operations which is not finitely generated.

Andrew Ng is a computer scientist and technology entrepreneur
He focuses on machine learning and artificial intelligence (AI)
Ng was a co-founder and head of Google Brain and the former Chief Scientist at Baidu

He is an adjunct professor at Stanford University and has made substantial contributions to the field of online education as the co-founder of Coursera and deeplearning.ai, teaching over 2.5 million students through his online courses
Ng has founded Landing AI, which provides AI-powered SaaS products, and launched the AI Fund, a $175-million investment fund for backing AI startups
He has won numerous awards for his work in AI, robotics, and related fields, and has authored over 300 publications
Ng is a strong believer in democratizing AI and lowering barriers to entry for businesses and developers. He also supports basic income to allow the unemployed to study AI to re-enter the workforce.

Friedrich Ludwig "Fritz" Bauer was a German pioneer of computer science and professor at the Technical University of Munich.
He studied mathematics and theoretical physics at Ludwig-Maximilians-Universität in Munich.
Bauer was involved in constructing computing machinery and proposed the widely used stack method of expression evaluation.
He was a member of committees that developed imperative computer programming languages ALGOL 58 and its successor ALGOL 60.
Bauer was an influential figure in establishing computer science as an independent subject in German universities.

He coined the term software engineering and defined it as the "Establishment and use of sound engineering principles to economically obtain software that is reliable and works on real machines efficiently."
Bauer's scientific contributions also spread from numerical analysis and fundamentals of interpretation and translation of programming languages to program transformation methods and systems.
Bauer was the doctoral advisor of 39 students.
He was one of the 19 founding members of the German Informatics Society and editor of the Informatik Spektrum.
Bauer received numerous awards and honorary doctorates.
The Technical University of Munich awards the Friedrich L. Bauer Prize in computer science in his honor.

Guy Lewis Steele Jr. is an American computer scientist who played a key role in designing and documenting several computer programming languages and technical standards.
He received his education from Harvard University and the Massachusetts Institute of Technology (MIT).
Steele worked at various organizations, including Carnegie Mellon University, Tartan Laboratories, Thinking Machines, Sun Microsystems, and Oracle.
He has contributed to the design of Lisp and its implementation, the Scheme language, the command set of Emacs, and the development of TeX.

He coauthored several books, including "The Hacker's Dictionary," "C: A Reference Manual," "Common Lisp the Language," "The High Performance Fortran Handbook," and "The Java Language Specification."

Steele received multiple awards and honors throughout his career, including the ACM Grace Murray Hopper Award and being named an ACM Fellow, a member of the National Academy of Engineering of the United States of America, and a Fellow of the American Academy of Arts and Sciences.
He is also a modern western square dancer and has published light verse and "Crunchly" cartoons under the pseudonym Great Quux.

Prof. Daniel Thalmann is a Swiss and Canadian computer scientist and a pioneer in virtual humans.
He is currently an Honorary Professor at EPFL, Switzerland and Director of Research Development at MIRALab Sarl in Geneva, Switzerland.
Thalmann earned a PhD in Computer Science from the University of Geneva in 1977 and was a Professor at the University of Montreal from 1977 to 1989.
He founded the virtual reality lab (VRlab) at EPFL, Switzerland and has been a Visiting Professor/ Researcher at several universities worldwide.
Thalmann has published more than 650 papers in graphics, animation, and virtual reality and is the co-editor-in-chief of the Journal of Computer Animation and Virtual Worlds.
His research focuses on realistic computer modelling and rendering of the human form, in motion, as well as behavioural animation of virtual humans and crowd simulation.
Thalmann coined the term Virtual Rehabilitation with Professor Grigore Burdea of Rutgers University and is a founder of the International Society of Virtual Rehabilitation.
He has received several awards and honors, including the Eurographics Distinguished Career Award in 2010 and the 2015 CGI Career Achievement Award.
Thalmann directed the short film Rendez-vous in Montreal, which is widely regarded as the first computer film to employ synthetic actors.

Carl Eddie Hewitt was an American computer scientist.
He designed the Planner programming language for automated planning and the actor model of concurrent computation.
Planner was the first programming language based on procedural plans invoked using pattern-directed invocation from assertions and goals.
The actor model influenced the development of the Scheme programming language, the π-calculus, and served as an inspiration for several other programming languages.
Hewitt obtained his PhD in mathematics at MIT in 1971.
He was a faculty member of the MIT Department of Electrical Engineering and Computer Science and became emeritus in 2000.
Hewitt's work on inconsistency robustness provided practical rigorous foundations for systems dealing with pervasively inconsistent information.
Planner introduced the notion of the "procedural embedding of knowledge", an alternative to the logical approach to knowledge encoding for artificial intelligence pioneered by John McCarthy.
The actor model of computation was introduced in a 1973 paper authored by Hewitt, Peter Bishop, and Richard Steiger and has been a focus of Hewitt's work for over 30 years.
A number of programming languages were developed to specifically implement the actor model, such as ACT-1, SALSA, Caltrop, E, and ActorScript.
Hewitt's selected works include PLANNER: A Language for Proving Theorems in Robots IJCAI'69 and The Scientific Community Metaphor IEEE Transactions on Systems, Man, and Cybernetics.

Maciej Stachowiak is a Polish American software developer.
He is currently employed by Apple Inc. as a leader of the development team responsible for the WebKit Framework.
Stachowiak was involved with the SCWM, GNOME, and Nautilus projects for Linux before joining Apple.
He is actively involved in the development of web standards and served as a co-chair of the World Wide Web Consortium's HTML 5 working group.
Stachowiak completed his education at MIT, where he worked on the Rethinking CS101 project.
From 1999 to 2001, he worked for Eazel and contributed to various Linux software projects, including Nautilus file manager.

Stachowiak announced the release of JavaScriptCore on a mailing list in 2002.

Samson Abramsky is a Professor of Computer Science at University College London.
He was previously the Christopher Strachey Professor of Computing at the University of Oxford from 2000 to 2021.

Abramsky has made contributions to domain theory, lazy lambda calculus, strictness analysis, concurrency theory, interaction categories, geometry of interaction, game semantics, and quantum computing.
He has applied methods from categorical semantics to finite model theory with applications to descriptive complexity.
Abramsky was educated at Hasmonean Grammar School for Boys, Hendon, King's College, Cambridge, and Queen Mary, University of London.
His research includes game semantics, domain theory in logical form, and categorical quantum mechanics.
Abramsky has published over 200 publications, including co-editing six volumes of Handbook of Logic in Computer Science.
He has won various awards such as the BCS Lovelace Medal in 2013 and the LiCS Test-of-Time award in 2007.
Abramsky is a Fellow of the Royal Society, Fellow of the Royal Society of Edinburgh, and Member of Academia Europaea.

His nomination for the Royal Society recognizes his contributions to the mathematical foundations of computation, particularly the development of game semantics and his generalization of Girard's geometry of interaction.

Donald Knuth is an American computer scientist, mathematician, and professor emeritus at Stanford University.
He is the 1974 recipient of the ACM Turing Award, informally considered the Nobel Prize of computer science.
Knuth has been called the "father of the analysis of algorithms" and is the author of the multi-volume work The Art of Computer Programming.
He created the TeX computer typesetting system, the related METAFONT font definition language and rendering system, and the Computer Modern family of typefaces.
Knuth created the WEB and CWEB computer programming systems designed to encourage and facilitate literate programming, and designed the MIX/MMIX instruction set architectures.
Knuth strongly opposes the granting of software patents, having expressed his opinion to the United States Patent and Trademark Office and European Patent Organisation.

Knuth is a writer and has written books on computer science and mathematics, as well as Surreal Numbers, a mathematical novelette on John Conway's set theory construction of an alternate system of numbers.
Knuth is an organist and a composer.
Knuth was diagnosed with prostate cancer in 2006 and underwent surgery.

Knuth is known for his humor, which includes paying a finder's fee for typographical errors in his books, intentionally referring to circular definitions in the index of his books, and proposing a satirical XML-based successor to TeX called "iTeX".
Knuth has received various awards and honors, including the Turing Award, the National Medal of Science, and the Kyoto Prize. He is a member of multiple academies of science and engineering.

Christopher Riche Evans was a British psychologist, computer scientist, and author.
He was born in Aberdyfi, Wales and educated at Christ College, Brecon.
Evans worked as a science journalist and writer before pursuing a B.A. in Psychology at University College London.
He worked on eye movements at the Physics Laboratory, University of Reading, and later at the Division of Computer Science, National Physical Laboratory, Teddington.
Evans wrote a book about the oncoming microcomputer revolution, The Mighty Micro: The Impact of the Computer Revolution, and scripted and presented a televised series on the topic.
He also wrote Cults of Unreason, a study of pseudoscience, and Landscapes of the Night: How and Why We Dream, as well as edited several anthologies and reference books.
Evans had a significant friendship and collaboration with writer J.G. Ballard.
He died of cancer in 1979 at the age of 48.

Ian J. Goodfellow is an American computer scientist, engineer, and executive.
He is most noted for his work on artificial neural networks and deep learning.
Goodfellow co-wrote the textbook Deep Learning and wrote the chapter on deep learning in the most popular textbook in the field of artificial intelligence.
Goodfellow obtained his B.S., M.S., and Ph.D. in computer science and machine learning.

W. Bruce Croft is a distinguished professor of computer science at the University of Massachusetts Amherst.
He is the founder of the Center for Intelligent Information Retrieval and served as the editor-in-chief of ACM Transactions on Information Systems from 1995 to 2002.
He has made major contributions to most areas of information retrieval, including pioneering work in clustering, passage retrieval, sentence retrieval, and distributed search.
Croft has led the development of one of the major approaches to modeling search: language modelling.
Croft and his research group have developed a series of search engines: InQuery, the Lemur toolkit, Indri, and Galago.
He is the Dean of the College of Information and Computer Sciences at the University of Massachusetts Amherst since 2015.
Croft has received several prestigious awards, including ACM Fellow, American Society for Information Science and Technology Research Award and IEEE Computer Society Technical Achievement Award.
He earned a bachelor's and a master's degree from Monash University and a Ph.D. from the University of Cambridge.

Barry William Boehm was an American software engineer and professor of computer science and industrial and systems engineering.
He was the founding director of the Center for Systems and Software Engineering at the University of Southern California.
Boehm's contributions to software engineering included the Constructive Cost Model (COCOMO), the spiral model of software development, and the Wideband Delphi method of estimation.

His research interests included software development process modeling, software requirements engineering, software architectures, software metrics and cost models, software engineering environments, and knowledge-based software engineering.
He received numerous awards, including the ACM Distinguished Research Award in Software Engineering in 1997 and the IEEE Simon Ramo Medal in 2010.
Boehm predicted in a 1973 report that software costs would overwhelm hardware costs, inspiring a change in direction in computing.
He published over 170 articles and several books, including Software Engineering Economics and Balancing Agility and Discipline.
Honorary degrees were conferred upon Boehm by the U. of Massachusetts in 2000 and by the Chinese Academy of Sciences in 2011.
In 1996, Boehm was elected as a member of the National Academy of Engineering for his contributions to computer and software architectures and models of cost, quality, and risk for aerospace systems.
Boehm passed away on August 20, 2022.

Honorary degrees were conferred upon Boehm by the U. of Massachusetts in 2000 and by the Chinese Academy of Sciences in 2011.
In 1996, Boehm was elected as a member of the National Academy of Engineering for his contributions to computer and software architectures and models of cost, quality, and risk for aerospace systems.
Boehm passed away on August 20, 2022.
Joseph Yehuda Halpern is a professor of computer science at Cornell University.
His research is focused on reasoning about knowledge and uncertainty.
He graduated from the University of Toronto with a B.S. in mathematics in 1975.
He earned a Ph.D. in mathematics from Harvard University in 1981.
Halpern has written three books on actual causality, reasoning about uncertainty, and reasoning about knowledge.
He won the Gödel Prize in theoretical computer science in 1997 and the Dijkstra Prize in distributed computing in 2009.
Halpern served as editor-in-chief of the Journal of the ACM from 1997 to 2003.
He was inducted as a Fellow of the Association for Computing Machinery in 2002 and an IEEE Fellow in 2012.
Halpern was awarded a Senior Fellowship of the Zukunftskolleg at the University of Konstanz in 2011.

He became a member of the National Academy of Engineering in 2019 for his methods of reasoning about knowledge, belief, and uncertainty and their applications to distributed computing and multiagent systems.
Halpern is the administrator for the Computing Research Repository and a moderator for subsections of arXiv.org.
His notable students include Nir Friedman, Daphne Koller, and Yoram Moses.

Dennis E. Wisnosky is an American consultant, writer, and former chief architect and chief technical officer of the US DoD Business Mission Area
He is known for being one of the creators and initiators of the Integrated Definition (IDEFs) language, a standard for modeling and analysis in management and business improvement efforts.

Wisnosky received his bachelor's degree in physics and mathematics from California University of Pennsylvania, a master's in management science from the University of Dayton, and a master's in electrical engineering from the University of Pittsburgh.
He founded Wizdom Systems in 1986 and later became the Chief Technical Officer for the Department of Defense (DoD) in August 2006
Wisnosky has published over 100 papers and authored or co-authored seven books

He is a frequent speaker on service-oriented architecture (SOA) and semantic technologies and has testified before subcommittees of both the U.S. House and Senate on U.S. productivity issues and the quality of American work life.

Gordon Villy Cormack is a professor at the University of Waterloo.
He co-invented Dynamic Markov Compression.
His research with Maura R. Grossman has been cited in legal cases in the US, Ireland, and the UK.
Since 2001, he has been a program committee member of The Text Retrieval Conference.
Cormack coached Waterloo's ACM International Collegiate Programming Contest team from 1997-2010.
He was a member of the International Olympiad in Informatics Scientific Committee from 2004-2011.

David G. Korn is an American UNIX programmer.
He is the author of the Korn shell (ksh), a command line interface/programming language.
Korn received his Ph.D. in applied mathematics from NYU's Courant Institute of Mathematical Sciences.
He worked at Bell Laboratories in 1976 and developed Korn shell in response to problems with Bourne shell and C shell.
The Korn shell was backward-compatible with Bourne shell and incorporated a choice of editing styles.
Microsoft once included a version of the Korn shell produced by Mortice Kern Systems (MKS) in a UNIX integration package for Windows NT.
Korn is also known as the creator of UWIN, an X/Open library for Win32 systems, and sfio, a library for managing I/O streams.
He became a Bell Labs fellow in 1984 and worked for AT&T Labs Research until 2013.
Korn retired from Google in early February 2018.
His parents were Florence and Nathaniel Korn, and he married Susan Lyn Weiner in 1967.
Korn's son Adam used to work at Goldman Sachs.

Kelsey Hightower is an American software engineer, developer advocate, and speaker.
He is known for his work with Kubernetes, open-source software, and cloud computing.
Hightower grew up in Long Beach, California and later moved to Atlanta, Georgia for high school.
He enrolled in Clayton State University but didn't continue because he found the technology courses lacking.
Hightower earned his CompTIA A+ information technology (IT) certification and got a job with BellSouth installing DSL service.
He started his own IT consultancy and later worked briefly at Google and Total Systems before joining Puppet as a software engineer in 2013.
Hightower briefly worked at a small Portland startup called Monsoon Commerce, at which he wrote confd, his open-source project.
He joined CoreOS in 2014 and began to contribute significantly to the Kubernetes project.
Hightower has worked for Google as an engineer and developer advocate in their cloud computing division since November 2015.
In 2014, Hightower became an active evangelist of Kubernetes and began to speak widely on the topic at developer conferences.
He co-founded the Kubernetes-focused conference KubeCon in 2015 and co-wrote a book with Kubernetes co-founders Joe Beda and Brendan Burns titled "Kubernetes Up and Running" in 2017.
Hightower was co-chair of the O'Reilly Open Source Convention in 2019 and on the governing board of the Cloud Native Computing Foundation.
He is a distinguished engineer, level 9 (L9) as an individual contributor, with Google Cloud as of October 2022.

Zohar Manna was an Israeli-American computer scientist and professor of computer science at Stanford University.
He earned his Bachelor of Science and Master of Science degrees from the Technion – Israel Institute of Technology and a PhD in computer science from Carnegie Mellon University.
Manna authored nine books, including The Mathematical Theory of Computation and co-authored an unfinished trilogy of textbooks on temporal logic and verification of reactive systems.
He received several awards, including induction as a Fellow of the Association for Computing Machinery and sharing the Herbrand Award with Richard Waldinger.
He supervised 30 doctoral students, including Nachum Dershowitz, Adi Shamir, and Thomas Henzinger.
Manna passed away on August 30, 2018.

John Yen is a professor of data science and leads the Laboratory of AI for Cyber Security at Penn State.
He was the founder and former Director of the Cancer Informatics Initiative at Penn State.
His current research goals are using AI and big data to address challenges in cybersecurity and advancing the frontier of artificial intelligence.

Yen has been a Principal investigator or co-Principal investigator on several multimillion-dollar research projects funded by organizations such as the National Science Foundation, Army Research Office, Office of Naval Research, and Department of Energy.
He is the lead inventor of the AI architecture R-CAST, which has been granted a U.S. patent and successfully applied to a real-world dynamic cyber-physical domain.
Yen received his Ph.D. from the University of California, Berkeley in 1986 and his B.S. degree from National Taiwan University in 1980.
He is the co-author of "Emergent Information Technologies and Enabling Policies for Counter-Terrorism" and "Fuzzy Logic: Intelligence, Control, and Information."
See also: Recognition primed decision.
External links: Dr. John Yen's website, the Cancer Informatics Initiative at Penn State, and Yen's Google Scholar profile.

Éva Tardos is a well-known Hungarian mathematician.
She is currently the Jacob Gould Schurman Professor of Computer Science at Cornell University.
Tardos's research focuses on designing and analyzing efficient methods for combinatorial optimization problems on graphs or networks.
Her recent work has delved into algorithmic game theory and simple auctions.
Tardos received her Dipl. Math and Ph.D. degrees from the Faculty of Sciences of the Eötvös Loránd University in 1981 and 1984, respectively, under András Frank's guidance.

She has won numerous honors and awards, including election to the National Academy of Engineering, the American Academy of Arts and Sciences, the National Academy of Sciences, and the American Philosophical Society.
Tardos is an ACM Fellow, a Fellow of INFORMS, and a Fellow of the American Mathematical Society.
She has received fellowships from Packard, Sloan Foundation, and Guggenheim.
Tardos has won multiple prizes, including the Fulkerson Prize, the George B. Dantzig Prize, the Van Wijngaarden Award, the Gödel Prize, and the EATCS Award.
Tardos is married to David Shmoys, and her younger brother is Gábor Tardos.
Tardos is also the author of the textbook Algorithm Design, co-written with Jon Kleinberg.

David Leigh Waltz was a computer scientist
He made significant contributions in several areas of artificial intelligence
He held positions in academia and industry
He was a professor of Computer Science at Columbia University at the time of his death
He received S.B., M.S., and Ph.D. degrees in Electrical Engineering from the Massachusetts Institute of Technology (MIT)
His Ph.D. dissertation initiated the field of constraint propagation
He worked on new methods for information retrieval involving comparisons to large amounts of data
He originated the field of memory-based reasoning branch of case-based reasoning

His research interests included massively parallel information retrieval, data mining, learning and automatic classification with applications in protein structure prediction, natural language processing, and machine learning applications applied to the electric power grid.
He served as president of the Association for the Advancement of Artificial Intelligence (AAAI) from 1997 to 1999
He was elected a Fellow of Association for the Advancement of Artificial Intelligence (AAAI) in 1990 and a Fellow of the Association for Computing Machinery in 1998
He died from brain cancer on March 22, 2012, at the age of 68.

Roberto Ierusalimschy is a Brazilian computer scientist.
He created the Lua programming language.
He holds a PhD in Computer Science from the Pontifical Catholic University of Rio de Janeiro and is a full professor of informatics there.
He did a post-doc at University of Waterloo in 1992 and was visiting professor at Stanford University in 2012.
He is the author of Programming in Lua and the creator of LPeg, a Lua library for implementing parsing expression grammars.
He created Building a Programming Language, a project-based learning program where students learn how to build a programming language from scratch.
There are several online resources for Roberto Ierusalimschy's publications and bibliography.

Margaret Elaine Hamilton is an American computer scientist, systems engineer, and business owner
She was the director of the Software Engineering Division of the MIT Instrumentation Laboratory, which developed on-board flight software for NASA's Apollo program
Hamilton has published more than 130 papers, proceedings, and reports and is one of the people credited with coining the term "software engineering"
In 2016, she received the Presidential Medal of Freedom from President Barack Obama for her work leading to the development of on-board flight software for NASA's Apollo Moon missions
Hamilton founded two software companies: Higher Order Software in 1976 and Hamilton Technologies in 1986 in Cambridge, Massachusetts
She played a critical role in getting humans to the Moon by developing systems software and designing and testing all on-board in-flight software for the Apollo spacecraft's Command and Lunar Module
Hamilton's innovations go beyond the feats of getting humans to the Moon and include legitimizing software development as an engineering discipline
Hamilton has received numerous awards including the Augusta Ada Lovelace Award, the NASA Exceptional Space Act Award, the Outstanding Alumni Award by Earlham College, and The Washington Award
Hamilton was inducted into the National Aviation Hall of Fame in Dayton, Ohio in 2022.

Timothy Wilking Finin is a professor of Computer Science and Electrical Engineering at the University of Maryland, Baltimore County.
His research focuses on artificial intelligence applications in information systems, including natural language processing, expert systems, multiagent systems, semantic web, and mobile computing.
Finin earned an undergraduate degree in Electrical Engineering from MIT and a PhD in Computer Science from the University of Illinois.
He has held positions at the Unisys Paoli Research Center, the University of Pennsylvania, and the MIT Artificial Intelligence Laboratory.
Finin is the author of over 450 refereed publications and has received research grants and contracts from various sources.
He has organized several major conferences, including the IEEE Conference on Artificial Intelligence for Applications and the International Semantic Web Conference.
Finin has received awards such as the Technical Achievement Award and the Presidential Research Professor title from UMBC.
He was named a fellow of the Association for the Advancement of Artificial Intelligence and an ACM Fellow for his contributions to knowledge sharing in distributed systems and the World Wide Web.

Dorothy Elizabeth Denning is an American information security researcher.
She is known for her work on lattice-based access control (LBAC), intrusion detection systems (IDS), and other cybersecurity innovations.
She has published four books and over 200 articles throughout her career.
Denning was inducted into the National Cyber Security Hall of Fame in 2012.
She is now Emeritus Distinguished Professor of Defense Analysis at the Naval Postgraduate School.
Denning earned a Bachelor of Arts degree in mathematics in 1967 followed by a Master of Arts degree in 1969 at the University of Michigan.
She received her PhD from Purdue University in 1975, her thesis focusing on secure information flow in computer systems.

Denning began her academic career as an assistant professor at Purdue University, then held positions at SRI International, Digital Equipment Corporation, and Georgetown University before joining the Naval Postgraduate School.
Her key innovations in computer security include the development of intrusion detection systems, encryption technology, and multilevel database security techniques.
Denning was involved in the controversial Clipper chip initiative to give the government authorized access to encrypted private communications through a key escrow system.

She has received over 20 awards for her contributions to computer security and was named an ACM Fellow, an ISSA distinguished fellow, and a fellow of the International Information Security Certification Consortium.
Denning and her husband, Peter J. Denning, were married in 1974 while she was working on her PhD at Purdue University.

Meir "Manny" Lehman was a professor at Middlesex University and Imperial College London.
He studied mathematics at Imperial College London and worked at Ferranti and Israel's Ministry of Defense before joining IBM's research division.
Lehman's research contributions include the early discovery of software evolution and Lehman's laws of software evolution.
He returned to Imperial College London in 1972 and remained there for 30 years before moving to Middlesex University in 2002.
Lehman was a Fellow of the Royal Academy of Engineering and the ACM, and received the Harlan D. Mills Award.
He passed away in Jerusalem in 2010.

Andrew James Viterbi is an electrical engineer and businessman.
He co-founded Qualcomm Inc. and invented the Viterbi algorithm.
He is the Presidential Chair Professor of Electrical Engineering at the University of Southern California's Viterbi School of Engineering.
He was born in Bergamo, Italy, and immigrated to the United States two years before World War II.
He attended the Boston Latin School and received both BS and MS in electrical engineering from Massachusetts Institute of Technology (MIT).
He carried out PhD studies in digital communications at the University of Southern California, where he graduated in 1963.
He proposed the Viterbi algorithm to decode convolutionally encoded data in 1967.
He also helped to develop the Code Division Multiple Access (CDMA) standard for cell phone networks.
He was the co-founder of Linkabit Corporation and Qualcomm Inc.

He received many awards and honors throughout his career, including the Benjamin Franklin Medal in Electrical Engineering in 2005, the National Medal of Science in 2008, and the IEEE Medal of Honor in 2010.
He was married to Erna Finci, a Jewish refugee from Sarajevo who was a Shoah survivor, and they had three children.

Bruce Schneier is an American cryptographer, computer security professional, privacy specialist, and writer.
He is a lecturer in public policy at the Harvard Kennedy School and a fellow at the Berkman Klein Center for Internet & Society.
Schneier is a board member of the Electronic Frontier Foundation and an advisory board member of several other organizations.
He has authored several books on general security topics, computer security, and cryptography.
Schneier also writes a monthly internet newsletter and maintains a security weblog.
He is frequently quoted in the press on security and cryptography issues.
Schneier has been involved in the development of several cryptographic algorithms.
He is critical of digital rights management and is a proponent of full disclosure in security issues.
Schneier has advocated for spending homeland security money on intelligence, investigation, and emergency response.
He coined the term "movie-plot threat" to describe dramatic terrorist attack scenarios and is a critic of security measures that protect against them.
Schneier believes that designing systems to fail well is more important than trying to prevent all malicious incursions.

Bjarne Stroustrup is a Danish computer scientist.
He is most notable for inventing and developing the C++ programming language.
He attended Aarhus University and graduated with a master's degree in mathematics and computer science.
He received a PhD in computer science from the University of Cambridge, where he studied communication in distributed computer systems.
He began his career as a member of technical staff at Bell Labs in 1979 and developed C++ there.

He has held various positions, including the head of AT&T Bell Labs' Large-scale Programming Research department and the College of Engineering Chair Professor in Computer Science at Texas A&M University.
As of July 2022, Stroustrup is a professor of Computer Science at Columbia University.
Stroustrup has written books and published over 100 academic articles on C++.

He has received numerous awards and honors, including The Charles Stark Draper Prize from The US National Academy of Engineering in 2018 for conceptualizing and developing the C++ programming language.
Stroustrup is a founding member of the C++ standards committee and has remained an active member ever since.

Daniel P. Siewiorek is an American computer engineer and computer scientist.
He is currently the Buhl University Professor Emeritus of Electrical and Computer Engineering and Computer Science at Carnegie Mellon University.
His research focuses on electronic design automation, reliability computing, and context aware mobile computing.
He has designed or been involved with the design of nine multiprocessor systems, and contributed to the dependability design of over two dozen commercial computing systems.
He has written nine textbooks and over 475 papers.
He served as Director of Carnegie Mellon University's Quality of Life Technology NSF Engineering Research Center and Department Head of the Human Computer Interaction Institute.
He was the former Chairman of the IEEE Technical Committee on Fault-Tolerant Computing and founding Chairman of the IEEE Technical Committee on Wearable Information Systems.
He is the recipient of the AAEE Terman Award, the IEEE/ACM Eckert–Mauchly Award, and the ACM SIGMOBILE Outstanding Contributions Award.
He was awarded the IEEE Computer Society Taylor L. Booth Education Award in February 2018.
He is a Fellow of IEEE, ACM, and AAAS, and was elected to the National Academy of Engineering in 2000.
He received his B.A. from the University of Michigan and his M.A. and Ph.D. from Stanford University, all in Electrical Engineering.

Eiiti Wada is a computer scientist and emeritus professor at the University of Tokyo.
He is the Research Director of Internet Initiative Japan (IIJ), a computer network technology company.

He became a member of IFIP WG2.1 in 1972, which specified, maintains, and supports ALGOL 60 and 68.
He was a member of the International Organization for Standardization (ISO) and contributed to character encoding set standards.

Peter John Landin was a British computer scientist.
He realized that the lambda calculus could be used to model a programming language, which is essential for functional programming and denotational semantics.
Landin worked as an assistant to Christopher Strachey from 1960 to 1964 before taking a position at Queen Mary University of London.
He was involved in the definition of the ALGOL programming language and international programming and informatics standards.

Landin invented the SECD machine, the first abstract machine for a functional programming language, and the ISWIM programming language, defining the Landin off-side rule and coining the term syntactic sugar.
He was involved with the Gay Liberation Front during the early 1970s and was once arrested as part of an anti-nuclear demonstration.
Landin's legacy includes an archive of material at the Bodleian Library in Oxford, an annual semantics seminar held in his memory, and a building at Queen Mary University of London named after him.
Some of Landin's selected publications include "The mechanical evaluation of expressions", "Correspondence between ALGOL 60 and Church's Lambda-notation", and "The next 700 programming languages".

Abhay Bhushan Pandey is an Indian computer scientist.
He made significant contributions to the development of the Internet TCP/IP architecture.
He is the author of the File Transfer Protocol and early versions of email protocols.
He graduated from the first batch of Indian Institute of Technology Kanpur in 1965 with a B.Tech in electrical engineering.
He received a Masters in electrical engineering and a degree in Management from the MIT Sloan School of Management.
He worked on developing FTP and email protocols for ARPANet and subsequent Internet.
He was a Director at the Institute of Engineering and Rural Technology in Allahabad and a senior manager in Engineering and Development of Xerox.
He was a co-founder of YieldUP International and Portola Communications.
He is currently chairman of Asquare Inc., Secretary of Indians for Collective Action and former President of the IIT-Kanpur Foundation.

Bernhard Schölkopf is a German computer scientist born on February 20, 1968.
He is known for his work in machine learning, especially on kernel methods and causality.
He is a director at the Max Planck Institute for Intelligent Systems in Tübingen, Germany, where he heads the Department of Empirical Inference.

Schölkopf is also an affiliated professor at ETH Zürich, honorary professor at the University of Tübingen and the Technical University Berlin, and chairman of the European Laboratory for Learning and Intelligent Systems (ELLIS).
He developed kernel support vector machine (SVM) methods and introduced kernel principal component analysis (PCA), which led to the foundation of the field of kernel methods.

Schölkopf also developed methods for causal inference and showed how to exploit underlying causal structures to make machine learning methods more robust with respect to distribution shifts and systematic errors.
He completed a Diplom in Physics and won the annual award of the German Informatics Association for his PhD thesis defended in 1997.
He founded the Department for Empirical Inference at the Max Planck Institute for Biological Cybernetics, which grew into a leading center for research in machine learning.
He is co-editor-in-Chief of the Journal of Machine Learning Research and among the world’s most cited computer scientists.

Schölkopf’s awards include the Royal Society Milner Award and the BBVA Foundation Frontiers of Knowledge Award in the Information and Communication Technologies category, shared with Isabelle Guyon and Vladimir Vapnik.

Steve Whittaker is a Professor in Human-Computer Interaction at the University of California Santa Cruz.
He is known for his research on computer mediated communication and personal information management.
He has worked in industry at Hewlett-Packard Labs, AT&T Labs, and IBM Research Labs.
Whittaker was born in Liverpool and studied Natural Sciences at Cambridge before obtaining his PhD at St. Andrews.
He is a Fellow of the ACM and a Member of the SIGCHI Academy.
Whittaker has received several awards for his work in HCI, including the ACM SIGCHI Lifetime Research Award and the ACM CSCW Lasting Impact Award.
He is also the Editor of the journal Human Computer Interaction.
Some of his notable publications include "Email overload: exploring personal information management of email" and "The Science of Managing Our Digital Stuff".

Andrey Nikolaevich Kolmogorov was a Soviet mathematician born in 1903.

He made significant contributions to the mathematics of probability theory, topology, intuitionistic logic, turbulence, classical mechanics, algorithmic information theory, and computational complexity.
Kolmogorov began his studies at Moscow State University in 1920 and gained international recognition in 1922.
He formed a lifelong friendship with fellow student Pavel Alexandrov and became interested in probability theory.
In 1933, Kolmogorov published "Foundations of the Theory of Probability," laying the modern axiomatic foundations of probability theory.
During World War II, Kolmogorov contributed to the Soviet war effort by applying statistical theory to artillery fire.
Kolmogorov married Anna Dmitrievna Egorova in 1942 and was actively involved in developing pedagogy for gifted children.
Kolmogorov received numerous awards and honors during and after his lifetime.
His legacy continues to influence the field of mathematics today.

Wim Ebbinkhuijsen is a retired Dutch computer scientist.
He is considered one of the "fathers of COBOL".
In 1979, he initiated the International ISO COBOL Working Group.
From 1967 to 2003, he was a member and chairman of the Dutch COBOL Committee.
From 1998 to 2001, he was a member of the NCITS/ANSI COBOL committee X3J4.
He has designed and rewritten many parts of the current COBOL standard.
Ebbinkhuijsen has worked for many years with Exin, a Dutch examination institute.
He has written many books about COBOL and wrote the first international standard for BASIC.
In 2004, he left the COBOL world after 42 years of commitment.
He received an IEEE award for his contribution to COBOL and was invested as a Knight of the Order of Orange-Nassau.
A valedictory symposium for him was held at the Vrije Universiteit of Amsterdam.
An external link to the symposium is provided.

Dora Stuart Primrose Metcalf was an entrepreneur, mathematician and computing pioneer.
She was born in Madras (now Chennai) in India to Irish parents and was the oldest of three children.
She won a scholarship to take an external degree with the University of London when she was 15 years old and obtained her Bachelor's degree in mathematics by the age of 19.
During World War I, she worked as a comptometer operator in a munitions factory and realised the potential of mechanical calculators.
In 1916, she was sent to Belfast to introduce comptometers into the shipyard offices and set up offices in Dublin and Cork in 1917.
In 1924, she founded Calculating And Statistical Services (CSS), which she ran successfully and provided international markets for information services using comptometers and tabulators.
She joined British Tabulating Machine Company (BTM) in 1934 and founded the Service Bureaux Division, while continuing to run CSS.

During World War II, BTM supplied "bombe" decryption machines to the codebreakers at Bletchley Park in England, and Metcalf was in charge of managing the supply contract but had to leave due to ill health.
She introduced the first electronic computer into Ireland in 1957 and retired in 1962.
She enjoyed fishing for salmon and trout with her husband, Commander John Savile Dent Metcalf, and they spent their final years in Otley in Yorkshire.
Dora Metcalf died in 1982 at the age of 90 and was buried in the Lawnswood Cemetery and Crematorium in Leeds in West Yorkshire.
