Michael J. T. Guy is a British computer scientist and mathematician.
He is known for his work on computer systems and his contributions to number theory, computer algebra, and the theory of polyhedra in higher dimensions.
He worked with John Horton Conway and is the son of Conway's collaborator Richard K. Guy.
With Conway, he found the complete solution to the Soma cube of Piet Hein and discovered the grand antiprism, an unusual uniform polychoron in four dimensions.
Guy's work includes contributions to geometry, number and game theory, and discrete mathematics.
He also worked on the sporadic groups.
Guy began work as a research student at the University of Cambridge, but did not complete a Ph.D.
He subsequently went into computer science and worked on the filing system for Titan, Cambridge's Atlas 2, and was co-author of ALGOL 68C.

Some of Guy's bibliography includes "Four-Dimensional Archimedean Polytopes" (1965), "On the Distribution of Values of Angles Determined by Coplanar Points" (1979), and "On rational Morley triangles" (2000).
A book called "The Symmetries of Things" mentions Guy's work on the grand antiprism.

Jie Wu is a Chinese computer scientist based in Pennsylvania.
He is the Associate Vice Provost for International Affairs and Director for Center for Networked Computing at Temple University.
Wu is also a Laura H. Carnell professor in the Department of Computer and Information Sciences.
He is noted for his research in routing for wired and wireless networks.
Wu served as Program Director of Networking Technology and Systems (NeTS) at the National Science Foundation from 2006 to 2008.
He has served as the General Chair and Program Chair of various IEEE conferences and is a Fellow of IEEE.
Wu has received multiple awards for his contributions to computer science, including the 2011 China Computer Federation (CCF) Overseas Outstanding Achievements Award.
He is currently a CCF Distinguished Speaker.
Wu resides in Fort Washington, Pennsylvania.

Michael Ralph Stonebraker is a computer scientist specializing in database systems.
Founder of several database companies including Ingres Corporation, Illustra, and StreamBase Systems.
Received the 2014 Turing Award for his contributions to database research.
Stonebraker's career can be divided into two phases: his time at UC Berkeley when he focused on relational DBMS and at MIT where he developed more novel data management techniques.
Notable projects include Ingres, Postgres, Cohera, Aurora, C-Store, Morpheus, H-Store, SciDB, and VoltDB.
Stonebraker has been a mentor to over 30 students including founders and CEOs of notable companies in the database industry.
Co-founder of Goby, a search engine for free time activities.

Mary Allen Wilkes is a lawyer, former computer programmer and logic designer.
She is known for her work with the LINC computer, now recognized as the world's first "personal computer."
Wilkes graduated from Wellesley College in 1959 where she majored in philosophy and theology.
She worked on the Speech Recognition Project at MIT's Lincoln Laboratory in Lexington, Massachusetts from 1959 to 1960.
Wilkes joined the Digital Computer Group, also at Lincoln Laboratory, just as work was beginning on the LINC design under Wesley A. Clark in June 1961.
Wilkes left the computer field in 1972 to attend the Harvard Law School and practiced as a trial lawyer for many years.

Her work has been recognized in Great Britain's National Museum of Computing's 2013 exhibition "Heroines of Computing" at Bletchley Park, and by the Heinz Nixdorf Museums Forum in Paderborn, Germany, in its 2015-16 exhibition, Am Anfang war Ada: Frauen in der Computergeschichte (In the beginning was Ada: Women in Computer History).

Some of her famous quotes include "I'll bet you don't have a computer in your living room." and "We had the quaint notion at the time that software should be completely, absolutely free of bugs. Unfortunately, it's a notion that never really quite caught on."

Sebastian Thrun is a German-American entrepreneur, educator, and computer scientist.
He is currently the CEO of Kitty Hawk Corporation and the chairman and co-founder of Udacity.
Thrun was previously a Google VP and Fellow and a Professor of Computer Science at Stanford University and Carnegie Mellon University.
He is also an adjunct professor at Stanford University and Georgia Tech.
Thrun led the development of the robotic vehicle Stanley, which won the 2005 DARPA Grand Challenge and is on exhibit at the Smithsonian Institution's National Museum of American History.
He also led the development of Google's self-driving car.
Thrun is known for his work on probabilistic algorithms for robotics, specifically in robotic mapping.
He was elected into the National Academy of Engineering and the Academy of Sciences Leopoldina in 2007.
The Guardian recognized him as one of 20 "fighters for internet freedom."
Thrun holds a Diplom and Ph.D. in computer science and statistics from the University of Bonn.
He co-founded the Master's Program in Automated Learning and Discovery at Carnegie Mellon University and later became the Finmeccanica Associate Professor of Computer Science and Robotics.
Thrun left CMU in 2003 to become an associate professor at Stanford University where he developed the robot Stanley, which won the DARPA Grand Challenge.
In 2011, Thrun joined Google as a Google Fellow and co-founded Udacity.
Thrun contributed to the area of probabilistic robotics, which uses statistics and robotics to make robots more accurate and efficient.
He has received numerous awards, including the inaugural AAAI Ed Feigenbaum Prize and the 2012 Smithsonian American Ingenuity Award in the Education category.

Gregor Kiczales is a computer scientist and full time professor at the University of British Columbia in Vancouver, Canada.
He is best known for developing the concept of aspect-oriented programming and the AspectJ extension to the Java programming language.
He is a co-author of the specification for the Common Lisp Object System and authored the book The Art of the Metaobject Protocol.
Kiczales' work focuses on allowing software engineers to create programs that look like their design to reduce complexity and improve software quality.
He started his research career at the MIT Lab for Computer Science and later joined the Xerox Palo Alto Research Center where he developed his most important research works.
Kiczales left PARC in 1999 to focus on computer science education and joined the Department of Computer Science at the University of British Columbia in 2000.
He designed CPSC 110, the introductory programming course in the UBC curriculum, which focuses on systematic program design by means of design recipes.
Kiczales co-founded Intentional Software with Charles Simonyi in 2002 but left in 2003 to return to UBC.
In 2012, he won the Senior AITO Dahl-Nygaard Prize for his work on the Common Lisp Object System and was named an ACM Fellow.
He was appointed executive director of UBC Extended Learning in July 2017.

Kiczales left PARC in 1999 to focus on computer science education and joined the Department of Computer Science at the University of British Columbia in 2000.
He designed CPSC 110, the introductory programming course in the UBC curriculum, which focuses on systematic program design by means of design recipes.
Kiczales co-founded Intentional Software with Charles Simonyi in 2002 but left in 2003 to return to UBC.

Jonathan Joseph James was an American hacker.
He was the first juvenile to be incarcerated in the United States for cybercrime. He was 15 years old at the time of his first offense and 16 years old when he was sentenced.

He committed a series of intrusions into various systems, including those of BellSouth, the Miami-Dade school system, and the Defense Threat Reduction Agency (DTRA) of the United States Department of Defense.

He was later found to have installed an unauthorized backdoor in a computer server in Dulles, Virginia, which allowed him to intercept over three thousand messages passing to and from DTRA employees and obtain numerous usernames and passwords of other DTRA employees, including at least 10 on official military computers. He also obtained the International Space Station's source code.
James was raided on January 26, 2000, by agents from the Department of Defense, NASA and the Pinecrest Police Dept.

He pleaded guilty to two counts of juvenile delinquency in exchange for a lenient sentence and was sentenced to seven months of house arrest and probation until the age of eighteen, required to write apology letters to NASA and the Department of Defense, and banned from using computers for recreational purposes. He later violated that probation, tested positive for drug use, and was taken into custody and served six months.

In 2008, the TJX department store chain was the victim of a massive computer systems intrusion that compromised the personal and credit information of millions of customers. James was investigated as a possible conspirator, but there was no evidence linking him to the intrusion. However, notes discovered during a search of his home revealed he had considered suicide.
On May 18, 2008, Jonathan James was found dead of a self-inflicted gunshot wound to the head.

Randolph Frederick Pausch was an American educator and professor of computer science, human-computer interaction, and design at Carnegie Mellon University.
Pausch was diagnosed with pancreatic cancer in 2006 and was given a terminal diagnosis in 2007.
He delivered a lecture titled "The Last Lecture: Really Achieving Your Childhood Dreams" which became a popular YouTube video and led to a book of the same name.
Pausch died of complications from pancreatic cancer in 2008.
Pausch received numerous awards and honors for his contributions to computing education and mentoring of women in CS and engineering.
The Randy Pausch Memorial Footbridge was built to link the Computer Science building and the Center for the Arts at CMU.
Pausch's book, The Last Lecture, became a New York Times best-seller and has been translated into 46 languages.
Pausch made appearances on The Oprah Winfrey Show and Good Morning America and filmed a cameo for the movie Star Trek.
Pausch received honors including being listed as one of Time's World's Top-100 Most Influential People and being named Pittsburgher of the Year 2008.

David A. Wagner is a professor of computer science at the University of California, Berkeley
He is a well-known researcher in cryptography and computer security
Wagner is a member of the Election Assistance Commission's Technical Guidelines Development Committee and the ACCURATE project
He received an A.B. in mathematics and a Ph.D. in computer science from Berkeley
Wagner has published two books and over 90 peer-reviewed scientific papers
Wagner served as a principal investigator for the source code review and documentation review of the California state Top-to-Bottom review of electronic voting systems certified for use
He has made notable achievements in cryptanalysis, including the discovery of flaws in WEP, A5/1, and Microsoft's PPTP tunnelling protocol
Wagner was involved in the development of the Twofish block cipher, which was a finalist for NIST's Advanced Encryption Standard competition
He also discovered a flaw in the implementation of SSL in Netscape Navigator in 1995.

Leonard Kleinrock is an American computer scientist and a professor at UCLA's Henry Samueli School of Engineering and Applied Science.
Kleinrock is best known for his work in queueing theory, which he applied to model delays in message switching networks in his Ph.D. thesis.
He later applied queueing theory to model the performance of packet switching networks, which played an influential role in the development of the ARPANET.
Kleinrock supervised graduate students who worked on communication protocols for internetworking, which led to the development of the Internet protocol suite.
His work on hierarchical routing in the late 1970s with student Farouk Kamoun remains critical to the operation of the Internet today.
Kleinrock has received numerous professional awards, including being elected as a member of the National Academy of Engineering and receiving the Draper Prize for the development of the Internet.

He has been inducted into the Internet Hall of Fame by the Internet Society and granted the 2014 BBVA Foundation Frontiers of Knowledge Award for his contributions to the theory and practical development of the Internet.

Joseph Frederick Traub was an American computer scientist and professor.
He held positions at Bell Laboratories, University of Washington, Carnegie Mellon, and Columbia, as well as sabbatical positions at various universities.
Traub authored or edited ten monographs and around 120 papers in several fields including computer science, mathematics, physics, finance, and economics.
He pioneered work on computational complexity applied to science problems and developed algorithms like the Jenkins-Traub Algorithm for Polynomial Zeros.
Traub was the founding Chair of the Computer Science and Telecommunications Board, National Academies and founding editor of the Annual Review of Computer Science.
He received multiple awards and honors, including the IEEE Emanuel R. Piore Award and the 1999 New York City Mayor's Award for Excellence in Science and Technology.
Traub donated some 100 boxes of archival material to Carnegie Mellon University Library.
He was married to author Pamela McCorduck and had two daughters.
His works have been cited 8,500 times as of November 2015, and he has an h-index of 35.

Marshall Kirk McKusick is a computer scientist known for his work on BSD UNIX.
He was the president of the USENIX Association and serves on the board.
He received his B.S. from Cornell University and his Ph.D. in computer science from the University of California, Berkeley.
McKusick is openly gay and married Eric Allman in 2013.
He contributed to the design of the Fast File System and implemented soft updates in FFS and UFS2.

Kristen Nygaard was a Norwegian computer scientist, programming language pioneer, and politician.
Nygaard co-invented object-oriented programming and the programming language Simula with Ole-Johan Dahl in the 1960s.
Nygaard and Dahl received the 2001 A. M. Turing Award for their contribution to computer science.
Nygaard worked at the Norwegian Defense Research Establishment and the Norwegian Computing Center, where he became the Director of Research.
Nygaard conducted research on planning, control, and data processing for Norwegian trade unions, as well as the social impact of computer technology and the general system description language DELTA.
Nygaard was a professor at Aarhus University and the University of Oslo, where he developed the Scandinavian School in System Development and the general object-oriented programming language BETA.
Nygaard received several awards and recognitions for his work, including the Norbert Wiener Award and the IEEE John von Neumann Medal.
Nygaard was also involved in Norwegian politics and activism, particularly against Norway's membership in the European Common Market and the European Union.
Nygaard died of a heart attack in 2002.

John Adam Presper Eckert Jr. was an American electrical engineer and computer pioneer
He designed the first general-purpose electronic digital computer (ENIAC) with John Mauchly
He presented the first course in computing topics, the Moore School Lectures
He founded the Eckert-Mauchly Computer Corporation
He designed the first commercial computer in the U.S., the UNIVAC
He developed the mercury delay-line memory
Eckert and Mauchly left the Moore School in 1946 over a dispute involving the assignment of claims on intellectual property developed at the University
They started the Electronic Control Company which built the Binary Automatic Computer (BINAC)
The Eckert-Mauchly Computer Corporation was formed, and they received an order to build the Universal Automatic Computer (UNIVAC), which was finished on December 21, 1950
Eckert received the Howard N. Potts Medal in 1949
He was awarded the National Medal of Science in 1968
Eckert believed that the widely adopted term "von Neumann architecture" should properly be known as the "Eckert architecture"
He died of leukemia in Bryn Mawr, Pennsylvania in 1995
In 2002, he was posthumously inducted into the National Inventors Hall of Fame.

Alston Scott Householder is an American mathematician.
He specialized in mathematical biology and numerical analysis.
He invented the Householder transformation and Householder's method.
Householder received a PhD from the University of Chicago in 1937 for his thesis on the calculus of variations.
He worked on mathematical biology with Nicolas Rashevsky at the University of Chicago, then shifted to numerical analysis while at Oak Ridge National Laboratory.
Householder contributed to the organisation of research, serving as president of the Society for Industrial and Applied Mathematics.
He was married twice and had two children.
Some of his selected works include "Discussion of a set of points in terms of their mutual distances", a pioneer paper in multidimensional scaling, and "The theory of matrices in numerical analysis".
Householder died in Malibu, California in 1993.
